summary: Cathy O'Neil explains her decision to undergo bariatric surgery, framing it as a quality-of-life choice rather than an aesthetic one. She openly states she loves her body and dreads the physical changes surgery will bring, but argues that long-term health concerns—particularly diabetes risk and its effects on the brain—outweigh her reservations. She lists practical considerations including her desire to bike in summer heat, family history of arthritis and joint problems, and the reality that doctors treat overweight patients poorly. The post sets up a future series examining the shaming tactics, financial incentives, and bad medical information she's encountered around the decision.
key_points: She values her body as it is and explicitly rejects the notion that weight loss equals self-improvement
Diabetes prevention is her primary motivation, especially its long-term effects on cognitive function
Practical mobility concerns—being unable to bike in summer heat—significantly impact her quality of life
She acknowledges systemic medical bias against overweight patients as a factor in her timing
The decision is framed pragmatically: why wait for inevitable joint surgeries when intervention now is safer
money_quotes: I have spent months trying to come to terms with that but I haven't yet.
I like my brain even more than I like my round body.
I felt like a prisoner all summer, cooped up inside the house and getting less and less fit.
Post-bariatric surgery patients complain about being cold, not hot. I'd rather be cold, because then I can wear one of my hand-knit sweaters outside while biking.
Doctors don't treat overweight people well.
I want to be one of those active grandmas that takes their grandchildren to the zoo, and then bikes home.
I don't want to be defeated by global warming, nor do I want to be forced to move to Maine.
key_insight: Medical decisions about one's body can be simultaneously an act of self-love and a pragmatic calculation about future quality of life, rejecting the false binary between body acceptance and health intervention.
